prompt:
You are a senior researcher in Medical Imaging / Biomedical Image Foundation Models.
Below are 11 important recent papers in this area, presented in random order. Each item contains only the paper title and a brief abstract; no lineage order, structured source-change fields, or evolutionary dynamics labels are provided.

**Frontier Question**:
RadVLM makes chest-X-ray interpretation conversational, but radiology practice is multimodal, longitudinal, and workflow-bound. What clinically deployable medical vision-language agent would integrate CT, MRI, X-ray, pathology, ultrasound, prior studies, reports, and EHR context while producing calibrated findings, grounded dialogue, and auditable evidence under real hospital workflow and safety constraints?

[Paper 1] Virchow: A Million-Slide Digital Pathology Foundation Model
Brief Abstract: Virchow: 632M-parameter ViT pathology foundation model trained on 1.5 million H&E whole slide images using DINOv2 self-supervised learning; enables clinical-grade pan-cancer detection with 0.949 AUC across 17 cancer types and 0.937 AUC on 7 rare cancers, demonstrating foundation models can achieve clinical-grade pathology performance.

[Paper 2] Segment Anything
Brief Abstract: SAM (Segment Anything Model): promptable foundation model for image segmentation trained on 1 billion masks from 11 million images; enables zero-shot segmentation via points, boxes, or text prompts without task-specific training.

[Paper 3] STU-Net: Scalable and Transferable Medical Image Segmentation Models Empowered by Large-Scale Supervised Pre-training
Brief Abstract: STU-Net: largest pre-trained medical image segmentation model (up to 1.4B parameters) based on scalable U-Net variants; pre-trained on the largest public medical segmentation dataset (>100K annotations from TotalSegmentator); demonstrates strong transferability across 14 downstream datasets covering diverse modalities.

[Paper 4] A foundation model for joint segmentation, detection and recognition of biomedical objects across nine modalities
Brief Abstract: BiomedParse: first biomedical foundation model that jointly performs segmentation, detection, and recognition for 82 object types across 9 imaging modalities using natural language descriptions as prompts; trained on 6 million image-mask-text triples.

[Paper 5] SAM 2: Segment Anything in Images and Videos
Brief Abstract: SAM 2: extends Segment Anything to video with a streaming memory architecture for temporal propagation; 6x faster than SAM on images with better accuracy; trained on SA-V dataset (largest video segmentation dataset); enables treating 3D medical volumes as video sequences for volumetric segmentation.

[Paper 6] Towards a general-purpose foundation model for computational pathology
Brief Abstract: UNI: general-purpose self-supervised pathology foundation model pre-trained on over 100 million tissue patches from 100,000+ diagnostic H&E whole slide images across 20 major tissue types; evaluated on 33 computational pathology tasks with new capabilities including resolution-agnostic classification and few-shot disease subtyping across 108 cancer types.

[Paper 7] CheXagent: Towards a Foundation Model for Chest X-Ray Interpretation
Brief Abstract: CheXagent: first vision-language foundation model specifically designed for comprehensive chest X-ray interpretation; introduces CheXinstruct (instruction-tuning dataset from 28 datasets), CheXbench (8-task benchmark), and demonstrates that AI-drafted reports improve radiologist writing efficiency in 81% of cases.

[Paper 8] Segment anything in medical images
Brief Abstract: MedSAM: adapts the Segment Anything Model to universal medical image segmentation by fine-tuning on 1.57 million image-mask pairs covering 10 imaging modalities and 30+ cancer types; demonstrates better accuracy and robustness than modality-specific specialist models.

[Paper 9] U-Net: Convolutional Networks for Biomedical Image Segmentation
Brief Abstract: U-Net: encoder-decoder architecture with skip connections for biomedical image segmentation; trains end-to-end from very few annotated images using heavy data augmentation; wins ISBI 2015 cell segmentation and tracking challenges.

[Paper 10] nnU-Net: a self-configuring method for deep learning-based biomedical image segmentation
Brief Abstract: nnU-Net: self-configuring framework that automatically adapts U-Net architecture, preprocessing, training, and post-processing for any new biomedical segmentation task; outperforms most specialized solutions on 23 international competition datasets without manual intervention.

[Paper 11] RadVLM: A Multitask Conversational Vision-Language Model for Radiology
Brief Abstract: RadVLM: 7B-parameter multitask conversational VLM for chest X-ray interpretation supporting report generation, abnormality classification, visual grounding, and multi-turn conversational interaction in a single unified model; trained on 1M+ instruction instances; achieves state-of-the-art conversational and grounding capabilities while remaining competitive across all radiology tasks.

Based on the frontier question and the papers above, generate a new research idea.

Return ONLY valid JSON. Do not wrap it in Markdown fences.
{
  "idea": "<short name of the proposed idea, 5-15 words>",
  "idea_description": "<150-250 words describing the full research idea>",
  "technical_plan": {
    "target_problem": "<specific problem, dataset, population, or phenomenon>",
    "core_method": "<technical method or mechanism, not a vague goal>",
    "limitation_addressed": "<frontier limitation repaired by this idea>",
    "expected_result": "<scientific result or claim if successful>"
  },
  "lineage_connection": {
    "source_ideas": ["<paper title or source idea used by this proposal>", "..."],
    "reused_parts": ["<specific method, evidence, data, or problem framing reused from the sources>", "..."],
    "changed_parts": ["<specific modified, replaced, combined, or newly added part>", "..."],
    "why_next_step": "<why this idea follows from the provided context>",
    "lineage_risk": "<most likely way this source connection could be wrong>"
  },
  "evaluation_plan": {
    "setup": "<concrete experiment, benchmark, proof, or analysis setup>",
    "baselines": ["<baseline>", "..."],
    "metrics": ["<metric>", "..."],
    "risks": ["<risk>", "..."]
  }
}
